natural_occurrence.description: Sodium hydroxide does not occur naturally. All production is industrial through electrolysis of sodium chloride solution.

manufacturing.description: Produced by the electrolysis of sodium chloride (salt) solution using the chloralkali process. This produces sodium hydroxide, chlorine gas, and hydrogen gas. The sodium hydroxide is concentrated and purified for various grades including food grade.

other_uses.cosmetics: pH adjuster in soaps (saponification), hair relaxers, chemical peels.
other_uses.industrial: Pulp and paper manufacturing, textiles, chemical production, petroleum refining, drain cleaners.
other_uses.household: Drain cleaners (industrial grade), soap making, pretzels.

eu.conditions: Used as processing aid; must be neutralized in final product. No free caustic remains in food.

usa.notes: Widely used in food processing, especially for pretzels and Asian noodles.

japan.notes: Common in ramen and noodle production.

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (no residue in finished food)

commonly_found_in: Soft PRETZELS (creates the brown, shiny crust)
Asian noodles - ramen, udon, lo mein (creates springy texture and yellow color)
Hominy and nixtamalized corn (for tortillas and masa)
fun_facts: THE secret ingredient that makes PRETZELS brown and shiny - the lye bath creates that distinctive pretzel crust
Creates the characteristic yellow color and springy, chewy texture in Asian ramen noodles
Known as 'LYE' when used in food - sounds scary but is completely safe when properly neutralized
Used for thousands of years to cure olives, making them edible (raw olives are incredibly bitter)
The same chemical reaction (saponification) used to make soap from fats
In nixtamalization, it unlocks niacin (vitamin B3) in corn, preventing pellagra in corn-based diets
Despite being highly caustic in pure form, it's essential for many traditional foods worldwide
